speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Leon Borovick is being interviewed. Mr. Munk said what? What else did he say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all I-- all remember is that. He asked me if I wanted to join.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any hesitation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think the purpose of the-- of joining would be? What was the purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew about the-- the underground people, would work the-- all kind of things. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their purpose to-- what-- what was their goal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't even ask questions. I just accepted that. But later, I-- they gave me-- they gave me work to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- tell us, how did they contact you after that? Who did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that they gave me a name of a girl, of a Jewish girl. Her name was Regina Kuperberg. I remember. I remember exactly her name, Regina Kuperberg. She said, you will go to the-- to the barbed wires, near the barbed wires where I worked. I worked near the barbed wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing near the barbed wires?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We were [? at-- ?] the-- the last couple of months in Birkenau, we had a group that didn't work at all. It was a fictitious group.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very interesting. It was called [INAUDIBLE] [? Lager. ?] We were supposed to clean the-- from the-- [? we ?] [? called it the ?] excrement, but we didn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a kapo and one of our people, who were nice men. We were five people only. There was-- [INAUDIBLE] made this-- made this group. It was called commando, a work-- a work-- a work group. And we just made believe we worked. We took something to carry this and that, but we didn't do anything. And that's how we survived without-- not working. So, we-- when you worked, you were exposed to-- to-- to these cruelties. Sometimes they used to beat you up and used to shoot you for nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, I went to the barbed wires and-- and I came-- and he gave me-- he told me-- he told me a word to say, like a [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't-- I don't remember to say something. You don't know the word?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I came over he said-- he said in Polish, [SPEAKING POLISH]. A girl came over near the-- [INAUDIBLE], widower. And then she took a-- something, a package. It was a package, like a handkerchief, in a handkerchief. Put-- and she threw that through-- through the barbed wire. She says catch it. And-- and I took it. I took it, put it in my pocket. When we went-- that was-- that-- that was the work. It was outside of the camp. That was-- no, it was in-- near-- it was in the camp. It was not-- in the barracks, not-- not-- it was far from the barracks. Then we had to march to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what did this package look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I never opened it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the size of the package?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never opened it. Interesting looking. It was like this, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was like this. Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it soft, or was it hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was not too hard. I think it was jewelry or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or maybe-- maybe ammunition. I don't know. Could-- could it be ammunition?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. What-- was it-- was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know. I never asked that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not hard to carry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was not heavy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not heavy.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was it hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard or was it soft?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. It was not-- it was not hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could it fit in your hand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I put in pocket-- in pocket.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was it bigger than your-- the palm of your hand? Could it fit?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bigger, I think. I can't remember this either.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it wasn't heavy?

speaker: interviewee
text: But all I know that I had to take this and give it at night when I used to come back to the camps, to the-- to the-- to my barrack there. And that time, now I-- I-- I say to myself, [? where ?] [? is ?] [? me? ?] What I did, I risked--I could have been-- been tortured to death if they caught me. But I didn't think that at that time about that. If they caught me, they would have tortured me to death. So-- but they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and I just want to go back for a minute to this package that you carried. How was it closed? How did they close the package?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was tied up like this. So, you [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: It was tied in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I don't remember. I just know I [INAUDIBLE] the package. I put it in a pocket. I came to the barracks. I give it to Shayek Erlich. He told me to give it-- to whom to give it. That was the routine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who were the people that you knew in the group? You knew Munk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew Shayek Erlich, these two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Shayek Erlich?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE]. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just three people.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those were the only three people you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a leading member. Not-- I had-- I had five cells under my supervision. The movement consisted of cells, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: The underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Consisted of cells.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were five people in a cell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean, 'in a cell'?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? It was ?] everything. So you has to be careful to-- everything was a secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does 'cell' mean? What do you mean by the word 'cell'?

speaker: interviewee
text: A group, OK. You call it-- you say group. We call it cells. You see, in the underground movement, even in-- even in other-- in the communist underground movements, in-- wherever they-- they have-- they have groups, little groups. Because it's-- it's a matter of being careful. You see, that's how it was. Five-- five people were under me.

speaker: interviewer
text: One woman. One woman, you, Munk, and Shayek. And who was the fifth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. These three. These three, I-- I was in contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I have-- I was a leader of 25 people. I knew five more people. See, each [? were ?] the five for the group of five. As I told you, there was cells. I was-- I was the leader of five cells, of five groups. Each group consisting of five people. Simple.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you rise to that leadership?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me that will-- that will be your function. They-- and they gave me the-- they gave me the names of these five people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times did you pick up packages from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times. I don't-- cannot tell you how many times. Many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it always the same kind of a package?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. Yeah, the same kind. The same. I never opened it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wonder what it could be?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never opened it. Interesting. Now I wonder much. And sometimes I think about it very intensively. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what did-- what did you hope to accomplish? Was it a question of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know this--

speaker: interviewer
text: --freeing people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- yeah, sure, sure. They did-- they did all kinds of useful things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to bribe-- bride the SS. They used to-- to-- to bribe everything. Maybe it was-- maybe it was a lot of jewelry, gold, a lot of valuable things. And maybe it was ammunition. Later, I heard they found-- they found a girl, four girls who were working for a-- in a-- in a factory. And they had ammunition there in the-- in the-- and they-- they had ammunition. And they were hanged, these four girls. It was known. In the-- in the-- it was in the press, in the literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of the-- of the people in the other cells?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- interesting. I think I told you that in New York, years ago, I was in-- in a-- in a cafeteria on-- on 96th Street, I think. That cafeteria [INAUDIBLE] I used to-- used to write about. I used to eat there. I live down not far from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one man came over to my table and said, oh, [? Leon. ?] You remember me? He stood next to me. Like this. Do you recognize me? And I said, who are you? I don't know you. Do you remember you used to tell us what to do? We used to sometimes-- we had-- we had to-- we used to gather all the five groups, the five cells with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go to a place where the Jewish doctors, they were also in a movement. They used to have to see us. We have to report to them without speaking. They was a sign how to do that. Sometimes we used to take the handkerchiefs and like make-- make, like we wipe our noses to show that we are-- we are active, that we are here. So I used to tell them. And they-- I was the leader of this group. And he recognized me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He recognized you after-- after 50 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't recognize him. He was-- I think he was from Bialystok. And I can't forgive myself for not taking his telephone number. So he was the only one to-- excuse me. I have the phlegm comes up. So that was I remember from that-- from this-- working in the underground. What was in the packages, I never opened it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell-- tell us some more, please, about the organization. You say that there were groups of five?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to have meetings too. We have to have meetings too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, so you actually-- you-- all the people who worked in the underground knew one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know, I say.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you knew five-- five cells.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were five people in each cell.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: But the rest I didn't. But I, see--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew 25 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: All these 25 people, we were just-- were marching to a certain place. We were working, making [? people ?] believe we are struggling. But I just saw him. I can't remember them. I don't even remember their names. You know what I mean? But I was in contact with these three people, Shayek Urlich, whom I gave this package. Regina Kuperberg, and the man-- the man who introduced me to that group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of those people captured? Any of those three leaders?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wonder. Yeah, I wonder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not that you know of.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were any of the other people in the cell? In each of your cells?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- [INAUDIBLE] Shayek Urlich, I think, was in Israel. I think-- [INAUDIBLE], I don't know. A few times they did-- they brought me even food later when I used to work before that. Shayek Urlich.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your understanding was that these packages would somehow make life easier for the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was work against the-- against the-- against the SS, against the-- against the--

speaker: interviewer
text: To free the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The German [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: --to help people escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a few people escaped. It was known, known in the literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: And oh, that people escaped. And did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- some made it and some didn't make it. Some came back and they were hanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you-- do you understand that the works of the underground helped these people to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Definitely. Definitely. The underground arranged all these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever ask if you could be one of those people to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. I would not-- I would not-- I would not-- I didn't have the energy for that. To escape was something-- was something extremely unusual to escape. It was took--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the people who did escape, how were they chosen by the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Interesting. We never asked questions. I never-- I never-- in a meeting with the-- with my superiors. Just to that man, the Munk. But we had meeting sometimes. We used to gather, and they told us the news about the-- the party-- the party Zionists or kind of things out there by the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of instructions did Munk give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and-- and my-- my work consist only of this thing. Only to give-- to deliver-- deliver the-- the package.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was your relationship to the other 25 men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there wasn't any relationship at all. There was-- I-- I only had to do it with the five people, you see. Five, the leaders of the-- of the cells, of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the five. And-- and you said that there are-- with the five cells--

speaker: interviewee
text: The five people, each one had-- had to-- took care of five.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know those other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not know their names even. I didn't know. I remember once we were marching to that place, as I told you, to-- to-- to show-- to be shown by the-- by the leadership, by the underground. All these 25 people. But one of them came to New York, and I didn't take his name. I can't forgive myself for that. He's the only one who-- who-- worked with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know what happened to the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He says you are-- you were my superior.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak about your experiences in the camp together when you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when this man--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were in the-- in the cafeteria, and then he left. We just spoke. It was at that time, I was teaching in-- and we had a really interesting-- an interesting life. I was a teacher. We had parties. And-- and I used to go down to Broadway. I like to go to the theaters. And the-- so I forgot that. I forgot about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the back of my mind, it was still there. But-- and-- and years went by, and I-- now I am closer to that-- that event. And I-- I think, where is that man? I wanted to give an ad in the papers to look for that man. But I don't know how to-- how to do that. Well, that man-- maybe he's not alive anymore. He's the only-- the only one. You don't know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- were there any-- were there any close calls for you? Was there any-- were you ever spotted? Were you ever seen? Were you afraid that you were going to be caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were caught? Tell us that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I told you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us that story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I took that package from under the barbed wire. And to my surprise, I noticed a SS man was next to me, standing. And he gave me a smack like-- like this. And he says to me in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. What are you doing here?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had that package right in my-- in my-- in my pocket. And I something-- and I said to him something which saved my life. What do you think should I-- should I have said? I said, do-- I-- I met my sister. I haven't seen her in a long time. That's all. Then he give me another smack in the face, said, go-- go away. And he didn't-- he didn't check your pocket?

speaker: interviewee
text: He could have-- he could have shot me, if he-- if he found this, I would-- I would have been tortured to death. But that the only-- the only time, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the signals for? You mentioned before a signal about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the first-- the first time when I met that girl. I had to tell her something. I don't remember that word.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said there was also a signal for-- for the medical doctors where you-- you took a tissue to your nose. What was that in reference to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was-- that was when we-- when we were-- we had to be seen for the-- for the leaders of the underground. I think there were doctors there. It was a place-- it was-- like kind of a hospital or something-- something. So-- so we had to do that. I remember that was the sign. To-- and that was-- that was a sign that we-- that we had from the movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- That man reminded me about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people who you knew to stay away from who might be collaborators?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I don't think of it. No one-- no one knew. Even my own brother didn't know. No one, but no one knew that. I-- I think I'm the only one who-- who belonged to the underground. I don't-- I don't-- I never even met a person who-- who was in the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Except this one man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never met a person who-- who-- who went-- who was operated on and they survived. I was operated in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain-- Tell that story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very sick. And when I was lying on the board where-- where we had to go to sleep. There were boards, you know. And-- with my brother, we-- with him together, my brother. In all the camps, we managed to be together in all the camps, in Dachau, in Kielbasin, in-- in Auschwitz, in [INAUDIBLE]. So felt-- I had a wound, and the pus was-- was-- was coming from my shoulder. And it touched the board. It was terrible. I had to wake up in the morning, and the-- and the-- and the-- and they oversee us to shower, did all kinds of [INAUDIBLE] to get up and to go to work. And sometimes, maybe, they used to beat us too. But not-- not too often. I remember here, and [INAUDIBLE], here was the pus. And I-- and I didn't know what to do. I became very, very weak. And I couldn't-- I couldn't work normally. So I didn't know what to do. So I went to a friend. His name was-- he was a doctor before the war-- Gordon, doctor-- I remember Dr. Gordon. Blessed his memory. He saved my life too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us what happened. What did Dr. Gordon do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me-- gave me advice. That advice saved my life. You see, he was not a doctor there, secretly. He was just like a-- a technician or something. They-- they had-- they used him for other purposes. He wasn't a real doctor, I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he examined me. He looked at that, and he said, you know what? You have only a few days to live. Remember, that what he said to me. You cannot-- don't-- I say, is there anything I can do? He says, yes. The only thing you can do, go voluntarily to that hospital. I think they call it ambulance in German. There was a little hospital. And they'll cure that. They cure that. I say, don't they send from there to the-- to the gas chambers? As far as we know, they send. I said, yes, they do. But lately, last couple of weeks, they didn't send. Maybe your-- maybe you take a chance. You-- you don't have anything you can do anyway. You're-- your-- you're dying. You're dying. So when I-- so like as if he said, take a chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother was in his barracks, and I went to the-- I couldn't wash myself because I was really sick. And I couldn't-- I couldn't do anything. I was a miserable man, the-- the lowest group in the camp. But you couldn't work normally. You couldn't talk normally. You were very, very weak, and you were about to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came to that time-- to that place and a Polish doctor told me to lay down, to count. I think I counted to 12. I think I remember. And he operated-- operate on me. I-- I feel like that-- that scar. And after the war, people told me, doctors told me they did a-- did a very good job. So then, that-- that's how the Germans did these [INAUDIBLE]. They-- they-- they operate. They-- they-- they cured you. And then they sent to the crematorium. They told me-- so I wanted-- after the operation, I knew I was alert. I knew if-- I had to go to work. If not, I'm dying. I-- I-- I says, are we allowed to go? He says, no, you're not going. So I took my-- I want to take to-- to get dressed. No, you're not-- not going to get-- to get dressed. You go like this, naked. Go to that number seven, block number seven. It's a barracks called number seven. I knew that was a death block, the famous block seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to that-- to that block. I remember it was, I think, in autumn time. It was cold. And then I was-- I was without-- without clothes. I was there. No one was there. It was a quiet, and abysmally quiet. And there was a little-- a little bed-- a little, like a-- a stretcher, I think. Like near hospitals there. I laid down there. And I felt so good. People-- people didn't-- no one beat me up, I-- I-- I expected to be-- not to be beaten up there. And I was left alone by myself. I was there on that place. I don't understand why people-- no one-- no one was around there. During that night-- during that night, my brother, again, went to that kapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kaminski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaminski. Trying after so many years, still. Still. So he-- and he cancelled. He-- that-- he took me out from the-- from the block seven to block eight. That was semi-- semi-death block. Where would I would get a chance to-- to live? The block seven, there was no [INAUDIBLE]. From there, they were taken to the crematorium. Over there, we have a few months lying there. And they-- they-- they cured me. That Kaminski came and said to the people-- to doctors, in Polish, you must-- you must save me this man. He is a friend of mine. I want to save that man. And they-- and they took care of me there. They bandaged and all this. And my brother used to bring me food sometimes. I was there in that camp. That was-- after that, when I became stronger, then I-- I joined the movement. But at that time, I was a Muselmann. I was-- I was a candidate-- a candidate for the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw just from far. I was working-- we were working at the crematories, a huge building. And we see from-- we see that-- that chimney, the-- the smoke. And we saw the people going. We saw people every day, every day, hundreds, thousands upon thousands marching to death. Marching, marching, marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men and women. And where they-- people saying in Polish, [POLISH]. They were going to the crematories. And the way to the crematory. They wave to us. They said, take care of yourself. In Polish, [SPEAKING POLISH]. They speak in Polish. I can't forget that. [POLISH]. Take care. Take care, brothers. Take care.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any-- was there any music or-- or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was music--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and music--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was music. There was-- There was a group who played music. People-- people went to the crematories, and they played music. There was-- there was a special group orchestra, and they played music for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who made up the orchestra?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't-- I never-- I think Jewish people too, the prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of music did they play?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kinds. Classic. They liked classic music. This was the [? best ?] [? chance ?] they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I was-- I was beaten. Do-- I remember one time I was-- I had to be beaten to death. That was my punishment. That was the rule.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you punished for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did something against the rule.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did something which no one, I think, no one of the-- of all these survivors that people-- that you will deal with did that. I was young. That was something-- I-- how I had the courage, I don't know. And I'll tell you how it was. We were-- we were-- we had to go to a certain-- to a house, a big house, to take a shower. I think it was every-- every week or every few weeks. Over there, we used to, of course, undress and to go that-- to the shower, take a shower. When I came there, well, a certain SS man came over to me. Usually waited-- waited many times, I went through that without any trouble. SS man came over to me. He saw me. And I was pale. I didn't-- that was the time when I was-- when I was-- when I was weak. He said to me, you're not going. You stay here. And I-- I found myself with a group of people, about 10, 12 people, standing. And the [INAUDIBLE], we took off-- we took off our jackets. And they give us a thermometer. I put the thermometer-- thermometer on here-- here. And he would-- and he says-- this SS man said to us, stay. I'll be back soon. And we all stayed. They stayed. 12-- about 12 people, 10, 12 people. I don't remember exactly but a group of people. At that time, I took the thermometer. And I saw I had the Fahrenheit was more than normal. I don't know how-- how much at that time was-- was-- it was normal, but I know it was-- it was above. So I said to myself, that-- that is not good. That-- that means that that group will be sent-- sent to the-- to the gas chamber. So what I did was I took the jacket. I threw out-- threw out the thermometer. [LAUGHS] And I ran. So imagine all the [INAUDIBLE]. A few hundred people in that-- in that maybe 200 or 300 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you run?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the-- to the-- to the barrack. And I figured I couldn't go in. See, but I-- I had to go with that group. So what'd I do? I said to myself, I'll wait till they will come to the march back, and I'll sneak in. Know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: The group that was taking the shower?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I mean, the whole people to the [? barrack. ?] That's what I did. And I succeeded. [LAUGHS] When we-- we are young, we are-- if I-- if I was older, 10, 20 years older, I wouldn't have done that. But at that time I was young. And-- so I-- I ran. I went-- when I was running, I thought the whole-- the whole [INAUDIBLE] people run after me, that the whole world is running-- is running after me. [? Imagine, ?] I broke the rule. But I didn't think-- I didn't think of it. That is-- that the punishment is death. The punishment is death. They-- they-- they kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came back, and I was-- I sneaked in. I went to-- to sleep at night. By the middle of the night, they called my name. They called names, all kind-- names of people to be punished. I was certain-- I forgot about this when I-- when I went to sleep. They-- they-- call out my name. The-- the-- they leader of the block, kind of a kapo. And he called me to his office. And he didn't say anything for a while. But I-- but I-- I knew that-- what I did. He said to me, lay down. And I laid down on-- on-- on a table, I think, somewhere. And he took a-- the stick, and he hit me, beat me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to count. One, two, and he gave me. The rule was that-- in many-- that kapo killed maybe hundreds of people. [INAUDIBLE] the same. We called them blockalteste, the block leader. The barracks we called block. So all of a sudden, he stopped-- he stopped beating me. He said to me-- I'll never forget his words exactly in Polish. He was a-- a Pole. I would have killed you to death. But I won't do that because your name. You have a nice Polish name. You see, Borovick is a Polish name. It's a mushroom, the name of a mushroom. He let me go. He saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same-- the same kapo, the same blockalteste who was cruel. He said himself-- the said himself-- I would have killed you. He was not a-- he was not a righteous man, see what I mean? But saved my life because he-- had a Polish name. I reminded him of a-- of his people. He let me go. That is what-- what I remember. Were you-- were you injured by-- by the-- the blows that he did give?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. What happened to the other people whose temperatures--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see them. I think they went to the crematories. See--

speaker: interviewer
text: They did go?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the common sense told me that went. Because they-- they-- if they took the thermometer, that means that-- it means-- it isn't for nothing. You looked too pale. I would be happy if I heard that they survived. But-- but I don't think they survived. Because I-- it was a few times, I did it. I sneaked out. So people didn't-- didn't think I really-- just-- I didn't do that. I remember a few times I stayed with a group of people. They told me, I didn't have-- didn't any group to go to work. That was terrible. You didn't have a group. So they-- they could take-- they could take you to crematories. So I stood the group people. And they-- and the-- and the SS says, wait. So people waited, and I didn't wait. I just went-- ran away, you see? That was how I saved myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have to be-- see, the reason that you couldn't-- you could not say that I survived because I was-- I was very alert, smart. No. But yet, you had to do something for yourself, know what I mean? Which I did. See? In addition to-- to the fact that people saved my life, I myself saved a few times, like-- like this thing, you know what I mean? And a few times, I just sneaked out. When did you get word about your family members, about your mother and what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- when I-- when I came to Birkenau, the same day when I came, the same-- the same moment when I-- when I reached out to place the platform, I knew. My-- my mother said to me-- to me, [INAUDIBLE]. And she went. And my sister went. And I knew they-- they went to the crematories. Same-- the same night. But-- and then I heard that-- that someone told me that even your Bashe that is in the camp, I saw her. One-- one-- a person whom I knew. And I was happy and-- and then-- to hear that. But later, my brother came, says to me, no. She's not anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your brother know?

speaker: interviewee
text: His-- his wife was working in a-- was in-- was-- knew her.
